metric_value: The plastic memory is related to other thin-film polymer memory approaches from the likes of Intel and Advanced Micro Devices.
notes: Kastner quote per Jay Lyman TechNewsWorld

metric_value: The biggest problem with today's high-density transistor memory is heat dissipation. If a polymer or plastic can't solve the electronic demands, it won't matter.
Cost and density advantages of polymer memory still do not address the larger issues for electronic memory — heat and leakage — that will be tackled in new DDR2 memory due out next year (2004).

The next generation of DVD-writing technology will use much less metal while still being able to support dual layers, which bodes well for the longevity of the technology — refuting predictions that polymer memory would end mainstream CD/DVD use.
DDR2 SDRAM became mainstream in 2005-2006 desktop and server memory — exactly the year Kastner forecast — and dominated until DDR3 (2007+) and DDR4 (2014+).

Polymer/plastic memory never reached commercial volume at mainstream density; Thin Film Electronics filed for bankruptcy/reorganization 2021 after three decades of effort; 3D XPoint/Optane briefly delivered persistent memory 2015-2022 then was discontinued — validating Kastner's 2003 skepticism.

CD/DVD media persisted in enterprise backup and consumer video through 2015-2020 and still exist in 2025 for archival and limited consumer use — outlasting the 2003-era plastic-memory-kills-optical narrative that Kastner refuted.
